summary:
The March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom on August 28, 1963, brought approximately 250,000 people to the National Mall in Washington, D.C., making it the largest political demonstration in American history to that point. The march was organized by civil rights and labor leaders including A. Philip Randolph, Bayard Rustin, and leaders of the major civil rights organizations. Its demands encompassed both racial equality and economic justice — an end to discrimination in employment and public accommodations, a federal jobs program, and civil rights legislation.

The day's most enduring moment came when Martin Luther King Jr. delivered his improvised conclusion to a prepared speech, describing his vision of racial harmony in America with the repeated phrase "I have a dream." The speech, broadcast live on national television, became one of the most celebrated in American history and crystallized the moral case for civil rights legislation in terms that resonated broadly across American society. Other speakers included John Lewis of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, who delivered an uncompromising critique of the pace of change.

President Kennedy met with march leaders at the White House and expressed support for civil rights legislation, which was then stalled in Congress. The march exerted enormous moral and political pressure for the passage of the [[event:3364|Civil Rights Act of 1964]]. It demonstrated the capacity of a mass multiracial coalition to mount orderly and dignified public protest on a massive scale, setting the standard for subsequent movements for political change.
